coredata.dc:title: Efficient photoelectrochemical aptasensing of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate in environmental samples based on N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanorods

coredata.dc:description: An efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform was developed for detection of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanorods (N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs) coupling with exonuclease I (Exo I)-assisted target recycling for remarkable signal amplification. N, S-GQDs uniformly grown on TiO2 NRs by simple hydrothermal method showed high electron-hole separation efficiency and superior photoelectric performance, which was explored as the photoactive substrate for anchoring anti-DEHP aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA). With the addition of DEHP, aptamer molecules fell from the electrode surface owing to the specific recognition of aptamer to DEHP, resulting in the increment of photocurrent signal. At this moment, Exo I could stimulate aptamer hydrolysis in the aptamer-DEHP complexes, so that DEHP was released from the complexes to take part in the next reaction cycling, which remarkably increasing the photocurrent response and achieving signal amplification. The designed PEC sensing platform exhibited excellent analytical performance for DEHP with a low detection limit of 0.1 pg L−1. Also, its applications in real samples were further investigated in detail. Thus, the established method would provide a simple and efficient tool for DEHP or other pollutants monitoring in the environment.

head.ce:title: Efficient photoelectrochemical aptasensing of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate in environmental samples based on N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanorods

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform was developed for detection of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots/TiO2 nanorods (N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs) coupling with exonuclease I (Exo I)-assisted target recycling for remarkable signal amplification. N, S-GQDs uniformly grown on TiO2 NRs by simple hydrothermal method showed high electron-hole separation efficiency and superior photoelectric performance, which was explored as the photoactive substrate for anchoring anti-DEHP aptamer and its complementary DNA (cDNA). With the addition of DEHP, aptamer molecules fell from the electrode surface owing to the specific recognition of aptamer to DEHP, resulting in the increment of photocurrent signal. At this moment, Exo I could stimulate aptamer hydrolysis in the aptamer-DEHP complexes, so that DEHP was released from the complexes to take part in the next reaction cycling, which remarkably increasing the photocurrent response and achieving signal amplification. The designed PEC sensing platform exhibited excellent analytical performance for DEHP with a low detection limit of 0.1 pg L−1. Also, its applications in real samples were further investigated in detail. Thus, the established method would provide a simple and efficient tool for DEHP or other pollutants monitoring in the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phthalic acid esters (PAEs) have been extensively used as plasticizers in a variety of industrial and household products to enhance their flexibility or ductility, becoming a kind of ubiquitous environmental contaminants [1–4]. Among PAEs, di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) is one of the most widely used plasticizers [5]. Because DEHP is physically combined with plastic polymer materials rather than chemically covalent, it is easy to migrate slowly into the surrounding environment such as soil, food and water [1,6,7]. Long-term exposure of human to DEHP even at very low concentration may cause reproductive system diseases, endocrine disruption and teratogenesis [1,3]. Therefore, it is indispensable to develop a rapid, highly reliable and ultrasensitive technique for detecting DEHP in the environment.

To date, considerable efforts have been devoted to detect DEHP by chromatography [8,9], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [10,11] and electrochemical (EC) methods [12,13]. Although these methods have high sensitivity, they suffer from low selectivity or peak discrimination in actual sample detection. To overcome these shortcomings, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [14] and molecular imprinting technique (MIT) [15] are applied. Nevertheless, ELISA is susceptible to the interference of environment and substrate, and the operation process is relatively complicated. MIT is also limited by poor binding force and difficult removal of template molecules. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) technique is a promising analytical method that inherits the advantages of both spectroscopy techniques and electrochemistry. Furthermore, the complete separation of photoexcitation and electrochemical detection reduces the background signal, greatly promoting its sensitivity [16]. However, the main flaw of PEC technique is lack of selectivity, which limits severely its practical applications. Aptamers, single-stranded artificial oligonucleotides that can selectively bind to targets [17,18] are ideal candidates for achieving selectivity during PEC measurements. But compared to biological macromolecules, the biorecognition interaction between pollutant small molecules with their aptamers is usually weaker, which is not enough to cause significant conformational changes and three-dimensional folding of aptamers [19,20]. As a result, it is a challenging task to realize the ultra-sensitive detection of low concentration DEHP in the environment through a weak detection signal. Exonuclease I (Exo I) can degrade single-stranded DNA from 3′ to 5′ terminal without degrading DNA containing secondary structures [21], so that it has been widely used in the design of various sensitive aptasensors to play a role of signal amplification. For instance, Xu et al. designed a biosensor based on Exo I specifically digesting aptamer in aptamer-malathion complexes, allowing malathion to continue binding with aptamer on the electrode, leading to a cascade signal amplification [22]. Chen et al. proposed a fluorescence sensing strategy with Exo I for uracil-DNA glycosylase (UDG) determination, where hairpin DNA can be turned on in the presence of UDG and hydrolyzed by Exo I to release 3′-6-carboxyfluorescein, thus turning on the fluorescence signal for sensitive detection of UDG [23]. Inspired by these works, we attempted to introduce Exo I into PEC sensing system to amplify detection signal to achieve ultra-sensitive detection of DEHP.

Additionally, selecting appropriate materials during the design of PEC sensing platform is another key factor in achieving excellent sensing performance [24]. As a widely used semiconductor, TiO2 possesses remarkable stability, eco-friendliness and low cost [25]. Among different TiO2 nanostructures, one-dimensional TiO2 nanorods (TiO2 NRs) exhibits large surface-to-volume area and excellent electrical transport performance [26]. But owing to its large band gap and high photogenerated charge recombine rates, the application of TiO2 is usually limited [27]. Modifying TiO2 with other narrow-band semiconductors is an available way to promote the visible-light harvesting ability and accelerate the separation of charge. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as green nanomaterials are expected to be the alternatives to toxic semiconductor nanocrystals such as CdS, ZnS and MoS2 [28,29]. Owing to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs exhibit strong light absorption, tunable electronic structure and florescence emission properties [30]. In particular, heteroatomic doping can effectively tune intrinsic electrical nature and optical properties of GQDs. As is known, electron-rich nitrogen doping shows greater electron mobility, introducing more chemically active sites [31], while the sulfur doping can generate huge defect sites and more edge sites in the carbon lattice due to the relatively larger atomic size of S than C [32]. Compared to single heteroatomic doping, co-doping can further adjust the properties of GQDs because of synergistic effects.

Herein, N, S co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs) grown in situ on TiO2 NRs (N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs) was prepared and served as a photoactive material for the first time to develop PEC sensing platform for the detection of DEHP. The N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs exhibited strong light absorption capability and prominent photoelectric activity. Meanwhile, the designed composites with large surface and good biocompatibility can offer large spaces for anchoring biomolecules, keeping good bioactivity and binding ability. During PEC sensing, aptamer bind to DEHP and fall off the electrode surface, resulting in the increase of PEC response. With the aid of Exo I, aptamer hydrolysis in the complexes and target DEHP cycling are initiated to achieve signal amplification. Thus, DEHP can be quantified according to that the photocurrent varies with the concentrations of DEHP. Besides, the detection performance of the PEC sensing platform for DEHP was explored in detail and its applications in actual environmental samples were assessed.
The materials and apparatus are provided in the Supplementary Material (SM).

N, S-GQDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal approach [33]. Citric acid (0.77 g, 4 mmol) and thiourea (0.92 g, 12 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. After stirring for 2 h, the mixture was hydrothermally treated at 160 °C for 8 h, followed by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 20 min, and then the supernatant was collected to obtain the final N, S-GQDs. For comparison, S-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) and N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were prepared by the same procedure, except that sulfur powder and urea were used instead of thiourea as sulfur and nitrogen sources, respectively. Undoped-GQDs (GQDs) were also synthesized using only citric acid.

TiO2 NRs was adopted from previous report with slight modifications [34]. First, FTO was immersed in acetone, ethanol and deionized water in sequence and cleaned ultrasonically for 15 min. 0.5 mL of titanium n-butoxide was added drop by drop into the solution containing 15 mL of deionized water and 15 mL of HCl, followed by stirring to obtain a homogeneous solution. The resulting solution was transferred to an autoclave and placed the conducting side of FTO facing up into it. The reactor was placed in an oven and reacted at 180 °C for 3 h, naturally cooled to room temperature. The dense white TiO2 NRs was evenly covered on FTO, followed by rinsing several times with deionized water and drying. Finally, the sample was calcined in air at 450 °C for 2 h to obtain crystallized TiO2 NRs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of undoped- and doped-GQDs/TiO2 NRs
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (0.77 g, 4 mmol) and thiourea (0.92 g, 12 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and stirred for 2 h. Then, the as-prepared TiO2 NRs was transferred together with the above mixed solution into a 50 mL autoclave, and reacted in an oven at 160 °C for 8 h. The as-prepared electrode was repeatedly rinsed with deionized water to remove the residual reagents and dried to obtain N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs. For comparison, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs and N-GQDs/TiO2 NRs were prepared by replacing thiourea using sulfur powder and urea as sulfur and nitrogen sources. GQDs/TiO2 NRs was obtained by reacting citric acid alone with TiO2 NRs.

The surface of N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs was encapsulated, leaving a reaction region of 1.0 cm2. 30 μL of chitosan aqueous solution (1%, w/v) was dripped on the reaction region. After drying, the electrode was rinsed sequentially with 0.1 M NaOH and deionized water. Then, 45 μL of glutaraldehyde solution (5%, w/v) was added to the electrode surface and reacted for 1 h. After rinsing with deionized water, 30 μL of cDNA (3 μM) was dripped onto aldehyde-functionalized N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs and incubated overnight at 4 °C. Subsequently, the electrode was treated with 30 μL (3%, w/v) of bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution to block the non-specific binding sites. Finally, the hybridization between cDNA and aptamer (30 μL, 3 μM) occurred on the electrode surface at 37 °C for 2 h to form the double strand DNA (dsDNA). The dsDNA-modified electrode was rinsed and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

PEC experiments were carried out in 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4). Using a 300 W Xe lamp (Beijing, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter to obtain visible-light illumination, and the distance from the light source to the working electrode was 30 cm. Mott-Schottky (M − S) experiments were conducted in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were operated in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution (5 mV s−1) to determine the conduction band minimum (CBM) and the valence band maximum (VBM) of the N, S-GQDs. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was implemented in 0.1 M KCl solution with 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in a potential range of −0.8 to 1.0 V, and a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were performed in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and 5 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] with the frequency ranging from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz at 5 mV.

During DEHP detection, the sensing platform was incubated with different concentrations of DEHP ranging from 0.1 pg L−1 to 10 pg L−1. Then, 25 U Exo I and 10 × Reaction buffer were added to the above solution and reacted at 37 °C for 2 h with slightly stirring to ensure the reaction completely. For selectivity tests, all interferents including 3,3′,4,4′-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB77), 2,3′,5,5′-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB72), benzyl butyl phthalate (BBP), dibutyl phthalate (DBP) and phthalate acid with the same concentration of 10 pg L−1 were used to replace DEHP.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 1 A displays that N, S-GQDs are evenly distributed and there is no apparent agglomeration phenomenon. Their average sizes are 3.6 ± 0.7 nm, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 1A. The lattice distance of 0.21 nm is clearly observed from the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of N, S-GQDs (Fig. 1B), which is related to the (100) plane of graphene [29]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N, S-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1C. The characteristic absorption peak center at ∼330 nm represents n-π* electronic transition due to existing the oxygenous functional groups (C–OH or CO) at the edge of N, S-GQDs [30,35]. Noticeably, the other two absorption bands at 422 nm and 595 nm are related to nitrogen and sulfur doping, which may be attributed to the n-π* of CN/C–N and SO [35,36]. Furthermore, it is observed clearly that N, S-GQDs show wider light absorption range extending to 450 nm, compared to GQDs without doping or N and S doping alone (Fig. S1). The introduction of nitrogen and sulfur increases the pathways of electron transition during absorption, playing an important role in regulating the electronic structure and optical properties of N, S-GQDs [29,37]. Fig. 1D is the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of N, S-GQDs. As the excitation wavelengths gradually increase from 320 nm to 520 nm, the emission wavelengths move from 455 nm to 560 nm, demonstrating the excitation wavelength dependence of N, S-GQDs. These PL behaviors may reflect the influence of different sized particles and the different emission sites distributed on each nanoparticle, which is a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based QDs [34,38]. And S-GQDs, N-GQDs and GQDs have similar excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behaviors as shown in Fig. S2.

Fig. 2 A and B displays the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of TiO2 NRs and N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs. The prepared TiO2 NRs with diameters ranging from 100 nm to 140 nm are grown densely and orderly on the FTO surface (Fig. 2A). However, when N, S-GQDs are loaded on TiO2 NRs, no obvious change is observed (Fig. 2B), which is owing to the relatively small size of QDs and could not be observed by SEM. The morphologies of TiO2 NRs before and after hydrothermal reaction in the solution containing N, S-GQDs were further observed by TEM. Fig. 2C demonstrates that TiO2 NRs exhibits a smooth rod-like structure with an average length of about 1.1 μm (Inset of Fig. 2C). The HRTEM image (Fig. 2D) reveals that TiO2 NRs has lattice fringes of 0.35 nm, belonging to the (101) crystal plane of rutile TiO2 [39]. After hydrothermal treatment, it can be observed that lots of N, S-GQDs are grown in situ on TiO2 NRs (Fig. 2E). What's more, the HRTEM image (Fig. 2F) displays that the new lattice fringes of 0.21 nm are present, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphene, illustrating the load of N, S-GQDs on TiO2 NRs. The energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis in Fig. 2G shows that N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs contains C, N, S, O and Ti elements. Furthermore, it can be observed from the elemental mappings that C, N and S are uniformly dispersed over TiO2 NRs.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis is shown in Fig. 3 A. A broad peak at 3000–3500 cm−1 is observed from the FT-IR spectrum of N, S-GQDs, which can be associated with O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, indicating that the surface of N, S-GQDs is rich in amino and hydroxyl groups. The peak at 2100 cm−1 is ascribed to S–H bond [38]. The strong bands at 1712 cm−1 and 1682 cm−1 come from the stretching vibration of CO in COOH and CONH. The bands at 1575 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 are ascribed to CC and C–N bending vibrations, respectively [30]. Weak peaks at 1093 cm−1 and 626 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CS and C–S [30]. For TiO2 NRs, the prominent peak at 500–850 cm−1 is the contribution of the vibration of Ti–O–Ti bonds [26]. The other two absorption bands at 3440 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 are agreed with the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups [26]. What's more, all the major characteristic peaks of N, S-GQDs and TiO2 NRs appear in the spectrum of N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs.

Raman spectra were performed to further confirm the load of N, S-GQDs on TiO2 NRs surface. As shown in Fig. 3B, the typical rutile TiO2 features at 143 cm−1 (Eg), 440 cm−1 (Eg), 610 cm−1 (A1g) and a multiphonon mode appeared at 230 cm−1 are observed in both samples [40,41]. When N, S-GQDs are grown on TiO2 NRs, the typical D and G bands of carbon-based materials appear prominently at 1350 cm−1 and 1558 cm−1 [41]. The large intensity of the D band indicates that the presence of huge defects in N, S-GQDs, which is ascribed to that N and S doping forms a more disordered structure of carbon-nitrogen (C–N) and carbon-sulfur (C–S) bonds [29]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses of TiO2 NRs and N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs were shown in Fig. S3. The diffraction peaks at 27.4° and 63.7° correspond to the (110) and (310) planes of rutile TiO2, while these peaks at 37.1°, 38.7°, 55.36°, 62.5° and 70.3°correspond to the (103), (112), (211), (204) and (220) planes of the anatase phase, respectively. Due to the low content and weak diffraction intensity of N, S-GQDs on TiO2 NRs, there is no diffraction peak of the graphitic lattice observed, which indicates that the load of N, S-GQDs has little effect on the structure of TiO2 NRs.

The chemical species of elements in N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs were studied with X-ray photoelectron energy spectra (XPS). Fig. 4 A distinctly shows the predominant signals of O, Ti and C elements. Besides, the presence of N 1s (400 eV) and S 2p (164 eV) peaks demonstrates N and S elements doping. The high-resolution Ti 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 4B, and the binding energy peaks at 458.6 eV and 464.4 eV are ascribed to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 [42]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4C) can be fitted with three peaks at 530 eV, 531.7 eV and 533.3 eV, which are associated with Ti–O, O–H and C–O bonding, respectively [43]. The C 1s peaks in the high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4D) appear at the binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.8 eV, which are characteristic of C–C/CC, C–N/C–S and CO bonding in N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs, respectively [30]. Two peaks at 399.8 eV and 401.1 eV in the N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4E) are related to pyrrolic/pyridinic and graphitic nitrogen signals of the N, S-GQDs [44]. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 4F) clearly shows three peaks at 163.2 eV, 164.7 eV and 168.7 eV, which represent the signals of S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2 and C−SOx, respectively [35].

The PL spectra of TiO2 NRs and N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs under the excitation wavelength of 320 nm are shown in Fig. 5 A. TiO2 NRs displays two emission peaks of similar intensity between 400 nm and 500 nm. The emission peak at 410 nm belongs to the free exciton emission of TiO2, while the peak at 455 nm is due to charge transfer transitions [44]. Fascinatingly, coupling N, S-GQDs with TiO2 NRs, the N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs composites exhibit the weakest PL emission intensity, compared with undoped or N and S doped alone-GQDs/TiO2 NRs (Fig. S4), demonstrating that N and S elements co-doping can greatly minimize the recombination rate of photoexcited carriers, improving the photoactivity and photoelectric conversion efficiency of the materials. Meanwhile, transient photocurrent measurements were performed (Fig. 5B). TiO2 NRs exhibits a very low photocurrent, which is attributed to that the photogenerated carriers in TiO2 can't be effectively excited under visible light. After loading N, S-GQDs on TiO2 NRs, the photocurrent density is apparently enhanced, and it is significantly higher than that of N-GQDs/TiO2 NRs or S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs (Fig. S5). This demonstrates N and S elements co-doping can well adjust the properties of GQDs and promote the separation of photogenerated charges of TiO2 NRs, further improving the photoelectric conversion efficiency. Meanwhile, the above results are well consistent with PL spectra. So N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs with the superior photoactivity and excellent photocurrent response can be considered as an appropriate material for PEC sensing.

The light absorption ability of the as-prepared samples was further investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). As shown in Fig. 5C, the absorption edge of TiO2 NRs is located at 400 nm. After coupling N, S-GQDs with TiO2 NRs, the absorption edge of N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs is slightly redshifted to 415 nm, and the visible-light absorption is enhanced [28,33], which is conducive to maintaining high activity of aptamer probe anchored on N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs. According to the equation: (αhυ) n = A(hυ-Eg), the band gap (Eg) values of TiO2 NRs and N, S-GQDs derived from the Tauc plots are determined as 3.00 eV and 2.60 eV (Inset of Fig. 5C).

M − S curves of TiO2 NRs are shown in Fig. 5D. TiO2 NRs exhibits the characteristics of n-type semiconductor, and the flat band potential (Efb) is −0.45 V. For an n-type semiconductor, its Efb is approximately equal to its conduction band (CB) [45], so the CB of TiO2 NRs is about −0.45 eV relative to Ag/AgCl electrode (vs. Ag/AgCl). According to the Nernst equation:

where Eo Ag/AgCl = 0.1976 V at 25 °C, and then it is calculated as −0.25 eV relative to normal hydrogen electrode (vs. NHE). According to the equation of EVB = ECB + Eg, the corresponding valence band (VB) value is 2.75 eV. Furthermore, LSV was conducted to determine the energy levels of N, S-GQDs and displayed in Fig. S6. The CBM and VBM potentials of N, S-GQDs can be obtained by horizontal intercepts extrapolated from the linear regions of cathodic and anodic scan results, and are around −1.30 eV and 1.30 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl), so the CBM and VBM values of N, S-GQDs are equivalent to −1.10 eV and 1.50 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Herein, the stepwise energy band alignment and the possible charge transfer mechanism of N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs are proposed, as displayed in Scheme 1 A. The electrons of the N, S-GQDs were first excited to its CB under the visible-light irradiation, and then injected into the CB of TiO2 NRs because of the matching energy band, followed by transferring to FTO and forming the photocurrent. The generated holes were transported in the opposite direction in the VB, engaging in reaction. Such an efficient charge transfer pathway helps to the separation of photoinduced carriers and promotes PEC activity of the material, which would greatly improve the analytical behaviors of the sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of the proposed PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1B shows the assembly process of PEC sensing platform and the sensing mechanism of DEHP. The N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs surface was first functionalized with the aldehyde groups (–CHO) by exploring chitosan and glutaraldehyde solution as the cross-linking agent. Subsequently, the terminal amino-modified cDNA was anchored on the composites by condensation reaction of –NH2 and –CHO. After that, anti-DEHP aptamer molecules were introduced onto the electrode by hybridization with cDNA to form dsDNA. The cross-sectional SEM image of the sensing platform is displayed in Fig. S7. The construction process of PEC sensing platform was investigated by Chronoamperometry, and the photocurrent densities of different steps were recorded, as shown in Fig. 6 A. TiO2 NRs exhibits a low photocurrent density of 2.4 μA cm−2 (curve a). It is worth noting that the photocurrent density of N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs significantly increases to 7.3 μA cm−2 (curve b), demonstrating that the combination of N, S-GQDs with TiO2 NRs helps to the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, improving the photocurrent response of the composites. When terminal amino-modified cDNA was fixed on the –CHO functionalized electrode, the photocurrent density dropped to 5.7 μA cm−2 (curve c) due to the weak charge-transfer ability of cDNA sequence. After blocking nonspecific sites, the photocurrent reduces slightly (curve d). After hybridization with aptamer, the PEC response continues to reduce to 5.0 μA cm−2 (curve e), suggesting the formation of a more rigid double helix dsDNA with lower conductivity further impedes electron transport. While the sensing platform was incubated with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 1.0 pg L−1 DEHP and 25 U Exo I, the photocurrent density remarkably increased to 6.1 μA cm−2 (curve f). This is because aptamer molecules dissociate from the dsDNA resulting from the specific recognition of aptamer towards DEHP reduces the electron resistance between the electrode and the solution. Thus, DEHP can be quantitatively monitored according to the change of photocurrent density.

Subsequently, each step of assembling the sensing platform was characterized by CV, as shown in Fig. 6B. The peak current of N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs (curve b) greatly increases compared with that of TiO2 NRs (curve a). It is attributed to that the modified N, S-GQDs improve the conductivity of the photoelectrode. When cDNA (curve c), BSA (curve d), and aptamer (curve e) were modified layer by layer on the surface of the material, the current decreased gradually. However, the current signal increases again after the incubation of the prepared sensing platform with DEHP (curve f), indicating that aptamer molecules fall from the photoelectrode.

EIS were performed to further study the stepwise modification process of the PEC sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 6C, compared with pristine TiO2 NRs, N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs has a smaller R ct value of 0.71 KΩ (curve b), which is ascribed to that the load of N, S-GQDs on TiO2 NRs fasts electron transfer. With cDNA assembled on N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs, the value of R ct drastically increases to 47.19 KΩ (curve c), which is due to the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphate groups on cDNA and the redox probe, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. A further increase in R ct value is observed using BSA as the blocking agent (curve d). With the aptamer molecules modified on the electrode surface, the R ct value increases evidently to 88.07 KΩ (curve e), indicating that aptamer molecules are successfully hybridized with cDNA, which greatly increases the resistance of the electron transport. In the presence of DEHP, the R ct value decreases to 71.97 KΩ (curve f), which can be explained from the release of aptamer molecules from the electrode surface owing to the strong affinity of aptamer toward DEHP, resulting in the decrement of the electron resistance. Therefore, the EIS results are accordance with the PEC responses and CV results, demonstrating the successful construction of the proposed sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of the PEC sensing platform for DEHP
ce:section.ce:para: Upon addition of DEHP, aptamer molecules dissociated from dsDNA on account of the specific identification of aptamer to its target, while the formed aptamer-DEHP complexes fell into the test solution and then digested by Exo I, releasing DEHP to participate in the next reaction. Thus, the charge transfer resistance of the sensing surface decreases resulting from the release of aptamer molecules from the electrode, so that the photocurrent density apparently increases. At the same time, the released target DEHP would recombine with other aptamer molecules, continuously triggering the next dissociation and digestion. In this way, small amounts of DEHP can trigger signal generation multiple times with the assistance of Exo Ⅰ, thereby achieving signal amplification and greatly improving the analytical performance of the sensing platform. Here, in order to prove the signal amplification effect of Exo I in the detection process, the analytical performance of the PEC sensing platform before and after the introduction of Exo I was studied (Fig. 6D). The photocurrent density of the sensing platform is about 4.3 μA cm−2 in the blank solution (curve a). When incubated with only 10 pg L−1 DEHP, it slightly rises to 4.8 μA cm−2 (curve b). The increase (ΔI 0) in the photocurrent density is 0.5 μA cm−2. After the introduction of Exo I into the test system, Exo I motivated aptamer hydrolysis in the DEHP-aptamer complexes, releasing the target DEHP that continued to bind to other aptamer molecules on the sensing surface. At this moment, the photocurrent signal rises significantly to 6.5 μA cm−2 (curve b′). The change (ΔI i) in the photocurrent density is 2.2 μA cm−2, which is four times more than the photocurrent response in only DEHP solution. The results confirm that the introduction of Exo I greatly increases the photocurrent response of the sensing platform, playing a role of signal amplification.

The sensing performance of the developed PEC sensing platform towards various concentrations of DEHP was investigated by I-t technique, as shown in Fig. 7 A. Obviously, as the concentrations of DEHP increase from 0.1 pg L−1 to 10 pg L−1, the photocurrent density gradually increases. After the concentration of DEHP exceeds 10 pg L−1, the photocurrent density almost no longer changes, suggesting that aptamer molecules are almost totally dissociated from the electrode. As shown in Fig. 7B, ΔI/I 0 has a good linear relationship with logarithm of DEHP concentrations (logC) in the range of 0.1 pg L−1 to 10 pg L−1, where ΔI is the change in photocurrent density after (I i) and before (I 0) recognizing DEHP (ΔI=I i−I 0). The fitting linear equation is ΔI/I 0 = 0.30304 + 0.19349 logC (R2 = 0.9962) and the limit of detection (LOD) for DEHP can be calculated as 0.1 pg L−1 based on 3σ/S, in which σ represents the standard deviation of blank sample signals, S stands for the slope of the linear equation.

For comparison, the analytical performance of several reported methods of DEHP detection is listed, as shown in Table 1 . Apparently, our proposed PEC sensing platform shows lower detection limit, demonstrating that it is promising in the determination of trace DEHP. The reasons for efficient PEC sensing of DEHP are as follows. Above all, the designed photoactive material N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs with good biocompatibility can provide a suitable microenvironment for immobilizing anti-DEHP aptamer and its cDNA, keeping good bioactivity of the biomolecules, which is beneficial for improving analytical behavior. Additionally, the as-prepared N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs effectively promotes the charge separation efficiency and enhances visible light absorption capacity, improving PEC activity and sensing response. Most importantly, the high packing density of aptamer molecules on the composites has a great influence on the sensing performance. Here, the load amount of aptamer molecules is directly related with the loading density of cDNA fixed on the electrode surface. The surface density of cDNA was determined by the chronocoulometric method, as displayed in Fig. 7C, and it is calculated as 6.59 × 1012 molecules cm−2. The detailed calculation is supplied in SM. Meanwhile, the N, S-GQDs-loaded FTO (N, S-GQDs/FTO) was modified with cDNA molecules for control experiments, and the packing density is 9.62 × 1011 molecules cm−2 (Fig. 7D). Apparently, the packing density on the proposed composites is more than six times that of N, S-GQDs/FTO, greatly improves the sensing performance.

In addition, some parameters affecting on the sensing behavior were studied before analysis. The photoelectric signal of N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs is critical to the sensing performance, which is mainly affected by the precursor solution concentration and hydrothermal reaction time. Here, the doping of nitrogen and sulfur derived from thiourea precursor, so the optimal amount of thiourea was explored and optimized to be 12 mmol (Fig. S8). What's more, the best hydrothermal time was investigated and 8 h was selected (Fig. S9). On this basis, the influence of thiourea amount on the signal of the entire sensing platform was further investigated, as shown in Fig. S10. When the thiourea amount was 12 mmol, the photocurrent signal of the sensing platform was still the highest, which helps to achieve highly sensitive detection of DEHP. The concentrations of cDNA that are related to the load amount of aptamer molecules were optimized, 3 μM of cDNA concentration was used (Fig. S11). The recognition time of aptamer and DEHP was investigated, and 40 min was selected (Fig. S12). As shown in Fig. S13 and S14, 25 U and 120 min were chosen as the optimal concentration and digestion time for Exo I, respectively.

In view of the possible interferences that may exist in complex environmental samples, the specificity was further explored by recording ΔI/I 0 value of the PEC sensing platform to the interferences, such as PCB77, PCB72, BBP, DBP, phthalate acid and their mixture. The concentration of DEHP is 1.0 pg L−1, while the concentrations of DBP, BBP, PCB77, PCB72 and phthalate acid are 10 times of DEHP. And the mixture contains 1.0 pg L−1 DEHP and other five interfering substances with 10-fold of DEHP concentration. As indicated in Fig. 8 A, the ΔI/I 0 value is about 0.31 for 1.0 pg L−1 DEHP, while it is less than 0.04 for other five interferences with 10 pg L−1 for each. Even the ΔI/I 0 value of the mixture is almost the same as that of DEHP alone. These results reveal that this sensing platform has good selectivity and anti-interference. This is mainly due to the specific affinity of aptamer and DEHP.

The reproducibility of PEC sensing platform was also investigated by measuring 0.1 pg L−1 DEHP with five photoelectrodes prepared under the same conditions (Fig. 8B), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.54% was obtained, indicating a good reproducibility. Furthermore, the stability is another key factor for the sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 8C, the photocurrent response is no obvious change after 30 continuous light on-and-off cycles. Meanwhile, the long-term stability of the sensing platform was studied by comparing the changes in photocurrent response for the storage of the sensing platform at 4 °C for 2 weeks. The result shows that the photocurrent reaches 91.8% of its initial value, indicating the excellent stability of the sensing platform.

The applicability of the sensing platform was studied in two environmental samples, including plastic bag and Fenhe water. The plastic bag samples were purchased from the local supermarket and cut into small pieces. 1 g of the samples were immersed in 40 mL methylene chloride, followed by adding 100 mL methanol to deposit the polymer material. The supernatant was centrifuged and filtered with 0.22 μm membrane. The Fenhe water samples collected from Fenhe River in Taiyuan, Shanxi Province were treated with a 0.22 μm filter to get rid of impurities. Finally, the two samples were spiked with certain concentrations of DEHP standard solution based on three experiments at each concentration. Furthermore, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was conducted to verify the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method. The detection results of the present sensing platform and HPLC are summarized in Table 2 . It can be intuitively concluded that the analysis results of the developed sensing platform are well consistent with HPLC. The recoveries obtained using the two methods range from 92.6% to 116.0% and RSD is less than 6.8%. So, the present sensing platform shows good accuracy and reliability, which can be applied to monitoring DEHP in environmental samples. Best of all, compared to HPLC, the proposed method doesn't need for complex pretreatment, tedious test processes and expensive equipment.

In summary, an efficient PEC sensing platform was developed for DEHP detection based on N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs coupling with Exo I-assisted target recycling for signal amplification. N, S-GQDs/TiO2 NRs dramatically promoted the charge separation efficiency and enhanced the visible-light absorption ability. Also, the introduction of Exo I played an important role of signal amplification, greatly improving the sensing behavior. Thus, the present platform exhibits excellent analytical performance for DEHP with high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility. Furthermore, it has been successfully applied to the evaluation of DEHP content in real samples. Therefore, the established PEC sensing method can provide a new and efficient strategy for the determination of DEHP and other pollutants in the environment.
